title: Stride: Controlling How Far the Filter Moves
concept: Stride sets how many pixels a convolution filter jumps between positions — stride 1 slides one pixel at a time for maximum overlap, while stride 2 skips every other position for a smaller, coarser output.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When a convolution filter slides over an image, what happens at each position it stops?

options.text: The filter's numbers are multiplied with the overlapping pixels and the results are summed into a single output value
The filter replaces the pixels underneath it with random noise
The filter deletes the pixels it overlaps

exercises.explain: Right — multiply and sum at every position. Today we ask: how far does the filter jump between those positions?

cards.title: Footsteps across the image
cards.body: Imagine walking across a room. You could take small, careful steps — or big, ground-covering strides. A convolution filter has the exact same choice as it crosses an image.

cards.title: What is stride?
cards.body: Stride is the number of pixels the filter shifts each time it moves to its next position. A stride of 1 moves the filter one pixel at a time. A stride of 2 skips a pixel between each stop.

cards.title: Baby steps vs. giant leaps
cards.body: Stride 1 is like tip-toeing across a room, checking every single tile. Stride 2 is like taking big leaps — you cover the same room, but you skip tiles and get there in half the steps.

cards.title: Counting stops on a 5x5 image
cards.body: Take a 5x5 image and a 3x3 filter. With stride 1, the filter can stop at 3 positions across and 3 down — a 3x3 grid of stops (9 total). With stride 2, it can only stop at 2 positions across and 2 down — a 2x2 grid of stops (4 total). Same filter, same image, half the stops.

cards.title: Stride isn't just 'faster'
cards.body: It's tempting to think stride only changes speed. It also changes what the filter sees: stride 1 lets neighboring filter positions overlap heavily, catching fine detail; stride 2 skips positions entirely, so some pixel combinations never get examined together.

cards.title: Stride 1 vs. Stride 2
cards.body: Same filter, same image, two very different journeys:

left.text:
Moves 1 pixel at a time
Dense overlap, big output
Catches fine detail

right.text:
Skips every other spot
Smaller, coarser output
Faster, less detail

exercises.prompt: A 3x3 filter slides over a 5x5 image with stride 2. Compared to stride 1, what happens to the output?

options.text: It becomes smaller, since the filter stops at fewer positions
It becomes larger, since the filter takes bigger jumps
It stays exactly the same size

exercises.explain: Right — bigger jumps mean fewer stopping points, so stride 2 produces a smaller output grid than stride 1.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A stride of 1 means the filter moves two pixels between each position.
exercises.explain: False — stride 1 means the filter moves exactly one pixel between positions, the smallest possible jump.

exercises.sentence: A stride of ___ makes the filter skip every other position, shrinking the output compared to stride 1.
exercises.explain: Stride 2 skips a pixel between each stop, so the filter visits fewer positions and produces a smaller output.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes what stride controls in a convolution?

options.text: How many pixels the filter moves between each position it's applied
How many color channels the image has
How the filter's numbers are chosen during training

exercises.explain: Stride is purely about movement — how far the filter jumps from one stop to the next.

exercises.prompt: You're building the first layer of a CNN for a security camera that needs to run fast on a tiny doorbell chip. You care more about speed than catching every fine detail. Should you lean toward stride 1 or stride 2, and why?

options.text: Stride 2 — bigger jumps mean fewer filter positions to compute, producing a smaller output faster, even though it captures less fine detail
Stride 1 — because stride never affects computation speed
Stride 2 — because it makes the output larger, which is always better
Stride 1 — because larger strides always improve accuracy

exercises.explain: Exactly. Stride 2 means fewer filter positions to compute, so it's faster and produces a smaller output — a real tradeoff between speed and detail. Next up: what happens at the edges of the image, and how padding fixes it.
